# Targets

<!-- generated by tools/sourcing/targets_board.py ‚Äî do not edit by hand -->

The unsorry worklist: theorems that are **already proven but not yet in mathlib**, vetted for absence and stated in Lean, waiting for an agent or a human to prove them. Claim one, open a PR, let the gates decide (see [Running an agent](../README.md#running-an-agent) and [ADR-012](adrs/ADR-012-Backlog-Sourcing.md)).

**1 open ¬∑ 97 proved ¬∑ 99 total prove-goals.**

| `sq-add-sq-eq-three-mul-sq` ‚Äî The Diophantine equation $x^2 + y^2 = 3z^2$ has only the trivial solution $x=y=z=0$ in integers. | blocked | 4 | ‚Äî | elementary number theory | Classic infinite descent argument modulo 3 showing that $3 \mid x$ and $3 \mid y$, which leads to infinite descent. |
| `abstract-regular-polyhedron-classification` ‚Äî For an abstract regular polyhedron ‚Äî V vertices, E edges, F faces that are p-gons, vertices of degree q ‚Äî with the two handshakes p¬∑F = 2E and q¬∑V = 2E and Euler's relation V + F = E + 2, the pair (p, q) is one of the five Platonic Schl√§fli pairs {(3,3),(3,4),(4,3),(3,5),(5,3)}. The classification (‚üπ) half of Freek #50 in combinatorial/Euler form. | proved | 3 | ‚Äî | Freek 100 (#50), combinatorial form (ADR-031 / SPEC-031-A, Track 1) | The classification half of 'there are exactly five Platonic solids', reusing the proved `platonic_schlafli_pairs` as keystone (Euler + handshake ‚üπ 1/p+1/q > 1/2 ‚üπ the five pairs). Coxeter, Regular Polytopes, Ch. 1. NOT the geometric Freek #50 (that is Track 2, gated on a mathlib polytope face lattice + Euler‚ÄìPoincar√©). |
| `alternating-sum-naturals` ‚Äî For every natural n, the sum over i in 0..n-1 of (-1)^i (i+1) equals -(n/2) if n is even and (n/2)+1 if n is odd (integer division over ‚Ñ§). | proved | 3 | [packet-ready](upstream/alternating-sum-naturals.md) | classic identities | Standard arithmetic alternating-series partial sums (1-2+3-4+...); tabulated in Hardy, Divergent Series, Ch. 1; elementary induction exercise in discrete-math texts. |

| `consecutive-triangular-eq-square` ‚Äî For every natural n, T‚Çô + T‚Çô‚Çã‚ÇÅ = n¬≤, where T‚Çô = ‚àë_{i‚â§n} i; the sum of two consecutive triangular numbers is a perfect square. | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | classic identities (triangular-number gems ‚Äî compounds on the Gauss sum) | Theon of Smyrna's classical observation that consecutive triangular numbers sum to a square (T‚Çô‚Çã‚ÇÅ + T‚Çô = n¬≤). Conway & Guy, The Book of Numbers; Heath, A History of Greek Mathematics. |
| `cube-eq-triangular-sq-diff` ‚Äî For every natural n, T‚Çô‚Çã‚ÇÅ¬≤ + n¬≥ = T‚Çô¬≤, where T‚Çô = ‚àë_{i‚â§n} i; equivalently n¬≥ = T‚Çô¬≤ ‚àí T‚Çô‚Çã‚ÇÅ¬≤, the per-term form of Nicomachus's theorem (the n-th cube is the n-th difference of squared triangular numbers). | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | classic identities (triangular-number gems ‚Äî the term-wise Nicomachus; compounds on `nicomachus-sum-cubes`) | The telescoping core of Nicomachus's identity ‚àëk¬≥ = (‚àëk)¬≤ = T‚Çô¬≤: each cube n¬≥ = T‚Çô¬≤ ‚àí T‚Çô‚Çã‚ÇÅ¬≤. Conway & Guy, The Book of Numbers; Mathematics in Lean ¬ß5 (the Nicomachus exercise). |
| `eight-triangular-add-one-eq-odd-sq` ‚Äî For every natural n, 8¬∑T‚Çô + 1 = (2n+1)¬≤, where T‚Çô = ‚àë_{i‚â§n} i is the n-th triangular number; the classic "8T+1 is a perfect (odd) square" test for triangular numbers. | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | classic identities (triangular-number gems ‚Äî compounds on the Gauss sum) | The triangular-number characterisation: m is triangular iff 8m+1 is a perfect square (the forward direction). Conway & Guy, The Book of Numbers; standard recreational/elementary number theory. |

| `fourth-power-mod-five` ‚Äî The fourth power of any natural number not divisible by 5 leaves remainder 1 on division by 5: if n % 5 ‚âÝ 0 then n‚Å¥ % 5 = 1. | proved | 3 | ‚Äî | classic identities (fourth-power congruence tower ‚Äî leaf; the Fermat case p=5) | Fermat's little theorem at the prime 5: a‚Å¥ ‚â° 1 (mod 5) for gcd(a,5)=1. Hardy & Wright, An Introduction to the Theory of Numbers. The mod-5 leaf that the others (mod 16, mod 3) do not cover. |

| `int-add-neg` ‚Äî Adding the negation of an integer yields zero. | proved | 1 | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | ‚Äî |

| `nat-add-comm-thm` ‚Äî Addition of natural numbers is commutative. | proved | 1 | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | ‚Äî |
| `nat-add-left-cancel` ‚Äî Left addition is cancellative on the naturals. | proved | 1 | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | ‚Äî |
| `nat-add-zero-thm` ‚Äî Zero is a right identity for natural-number addition. | proved | 1 | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | ‚Äî |
| `nat-dvd-refl` ‚Äî Divisibility is reflexive on the naturals. | proved | 1 | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | ‚Äî |
| `nat-even-or-odd` ‚Äî Every natural number is even or odd. | proved | 1 | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | ‚Äî |
| `nat-gcd-comm` ‚Äî The gcd of two naturals is symmetric. | proved | 1 | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | ‚Äî |
| `nat-le-add-right` ‚Äî A natural number is at most itself plus another. | proved | 1 | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | ‚Äî |
| `nat-le-succ` ‚Äî Every natural number is at most its successor. | proved | 1 | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | ‚Äî |
| `nat-mul-add-distrib` ‚Äî Multiplication distributes over addition on the left for naturals. | proved | 1 | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | ‚Äî |
| `nat-mul-comm-thm` ‚Äî Multiplication of natural numbers is commutative. | proved | 1 | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | ‚Äî |
| `nat-mul-one-thm` ‚Äî One is a right identity for natural-number multiplication. | proved | 1 | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | ‚Äî |
| `nat-mul-zero-thm` ‚Äî Any natural number times zero is zero. | proved | 1 | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | ‚Äî |

| `nicomachus-sum-cubes` ‚Äî Nicomachus's theorem: the sum of the first n cubes equals the square of the sum | proved | 3 | [packet-ready](upstream/nicomachus-sum-cubes.md) | Phase-2 seeded target (pre-ADR-012) | Nicomachus of Gerasa, *Introduction to Arithmetic* II.20; left as a reader exercise in *Mathematics in Lean* ¬ß5 |
| `nicomachus-sum-cubes-triangular` ‚Äî For every natural n, the sum over i in 0..n of i^3 equals (n(n+1)/2)^2 (the explicit triangular-number form of Nicomachus' theorem). | proved | 3 | [packet-ready](upstream/nicomachus-sum-cubes-triangular.md) | Freek 100 / classic | Nicomachus of Gerasa, Introduction to Arithmetic, Book II, in explicit closed form; Graham, Knuth & Patashnik, Concrete Mathematics, ¬ß2.5 (‚àëk^3 = (n(n+1)/2)^2). |
| `not-prime-pow-four-add-four` ‚Äî For every natural n with n > 1, n^4 + 4 is not prime, via the Sophie Germain factorization n^4+4 = (n^2-2n+2)(n^2+2n+2). | proved | 3 | ‚Äî | classic identities | Sophie Germain's identity, compositeness corollary. Sierpi≈Ñski, Elementary Theory of Numbers (PWN/North-Holland, 1988); standard olympiad result. |
| `odd-fourth-power-mod-sixteen` ‚Äî The fourth power of every odd natural number leaves remainder 1 on division by 16: if n is odd then n‚Å¥ % 16 = 1. | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | classic identities (fourth-power congruence tower ‚Äî leaf; compounds on `odd-sq-mod-eight`) | Standard elementary number theory: odd squares are ‚â° 1 (mod 8), so odd fourth powers are ‚â° 1 (mod 16). Hardy & Wright, An Introduction to the Theory of Numbers (quadratic-residue preliminaries). One power up from the proved `odd-sq-mod-eight`. |
| `odd-sq-mod-eight` ‚Äî The square of every odd natural number leaves remainder 1 on division by 8: if n is odd then n^2 % 8 = 1. | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | classic identities | Odd squares are ‚â° 1 (mod 8); Hardy & Wright, An Introduction to the Theory of Numbers (quadratic-residue preliminaries); standard elementary number theory. |
| `one-add-four-b-fourth-not-prime` ‚Äî For every natural b > 1, 1 + 4b‚Å¥ is not prime; by the Sophie Germain factorization 1 + 4b‚Å¥ = (2b¬≤+2b+1)(2b¬≤‚àí2b+1), with both factors exceeding 1. | proved | 3 | ‚Äî | classic identities (compositeness-via-factorization ‚Äî the Sophie Germain a=1 corollary) | The a=1 case of Sophie Germain's identity a‚Å¥+4b‚Å¥ = (a¬≤‚àí2ab+2b¬≤)(a¬≤+2ab+2b¬≤); a companion to the proved `not-prime-pow-four-add-four` (which is the b=1 case n‚Å¥+4). Sierpi≈Ñski, Elementary Theory of Numbers. |

| `pow-four-add-sq-add-one-factor` ‚Äî For every integer n, n‚Å¥ + n¬≤ + 1 = (n¬≤ + n + 1)(n¬≤ ‚àí n + 1); the classic Aurifeuillian-style factorization of x‚Å¥+x¬≤+1 (a product of the two "cyclotomic-like" quadratics). | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | classic identities (compositeness-via-factorization ‚Äî the factorization leaf) | Standard algebra: x‚Å¥+x¬≤+1 = (x¬≤+1)¬≤‚àíx¬≤ = (x¬≤+x+1)(x¬≤‚àíx+1). Appears throughout olympiad number theory as the lever for showing n‚Å¥+n¬≤+1 is composite. Engel, Problem-Solving Strategies; Andreescu & Andrica, Number Theory. |
| `pow-four-add-sq-add-one-not-prime` ‚Äî For every natural n > 1, n‚Å¥ + n¬≤ + 1 is not prime; it factors as (n¬≤+n+1)(n¬≤‚àín+1) with both factors exceeding 1. | proved | 3 | ‚Äî | classic identities (compositeness-via-factorization ‚Äî the **capstone**; compounds on `pow-four-add-sq-add-one-factor`) | Classic olympiad compositeness result, the x‚Å¥+x¬≤+1 analogue of the proved `not-prime-pow-four-add-four` (Sophie Germain). Engel, Problem-Solving Strategies (divisibility/compositeness). |
| `prime-fourth-power-mod-240` ‚Äî The fourth power of every prime p > 5 leaves remainder 1 on division by 240: if p is prime and p > 5 then p‚Å¥ % 240 = 1. | proved | 4 | ‚Äî | classic identities (fourth-power congruence tower ‚Äî **root**; deps: the mod-16, mod-3, mod-5 leaves) | Classic competition gem "240 ‚à£ p‚Å¥ ‚àí 1 for every prime p > 5", one power up from the proved-here "24 ‚à£ p¬≤ ‚àí 1 for primes p > 3" (binto-labs, `prime-sq-mod-twenty-four`). 240 = 16¬∑3¬∑5; the result is CRT over the three coprime moduli. Sierpi≈Ñski, Elementary Theory of Numbers; standard olympiad result. |
| `prime-sq-mod-twenty-four` ‚Äî The square of every prime p > 3 leaves remainder 1 on division by 24: if p is prime and p > 3 then p^2 % 24 = 1. | proved | 3 | ‚Äî | classic identities (thread-B depth-chain mid; deps: odd-sq-mod-eight, sq-mod-three) | Classic chestnut "24 divides p¬≤ ‚àí 1 for every prime p > 3"; Sierpi≈Ñski, Elementary Theory of Numbers; standard olympiad/intro-NT result via CRT from the mod-8 and mod-3 facts. |
| `prime-sq-sub-sq-div-twenty-four` ‚Äî For any two primes p, q both greater than 3, 24 divides p^2 - q^2 (stated over ‚Ñ§). | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | classic identities (thread-B depth-chain root; deps: prime-sq-mod-twenty-four) | Standard corollary of "p¬≤ ‚â° 1 (mod 24) for primes p > 3"; Sierpi≈Ñski, Elementary Theory of Numbers; common olympiad exercise. |
| `six-dvd-three-consecutive` ‚Äî For every natural n, 6 ‚à£ n(n+1)(n+2): the product of three consecutive integers is divisible by 6. | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | elementary number theory (product of k consecutive integers is divisible by k!) | Hardy & Wright, An Introduction to the Theory of Numbers, ¬ß6 ‚Äî the k=3 case. mathlib has `Nat.factorial_dvd_descFactorial` but no standalone `6 ‚à£ n(n+1)(n+2)`. |

| `sum-range-choose-mul-two-pow` ‚Äî For every natural n, the sum over k in 0..n of C(n,k)¬∑2·µè equals 3‚Åø; the binomial theorem at x=2: ‚àëC(n,k)2·µè = (1+2)‚Åø = 3‚Åø. | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | classic identities (binomial-moment tower ‚Äî the weighted row sum) | Specialisation of the binomial theorem (1+x)‚Åø = ‚àëC(n,k)x·µè at x=2. Standard; Concrete Mathematics, Ch. 5. |
| `sum-range-cube-even` ‚Äî For every natural n, the sum of the cubes of the first n even numbers equals 2n¬≤(n‚àí1)¬≤; i.e. (2¬∑0)¬≥+2¬≥+4¬≥+‚Ä¶+(2(n‚àí1))¬≥ = 2n¬≤(n‚àí1)¬≤. | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | classic identities (power-sum tower ‚Äî compounds on `nicomachus-sum-cubes`) | Even-cube sums ‚àë(2k)¬≥ = 8‚àëk¬≥ = 8¬∑(n(n‚àí1)/2)¬≤; immediate corollary of Nicomachus's theorem (‚àëk¬≥ = (‚àëk)¬≤) scaled by 8. CRC Standard Mathematical Tables (sums of powers); companion of the proved `sum-range-cube-odd`. |
| `sum-range-cube-mul-choose` ‚Äî For every natural n, 8¬∑(sum of k¬≥¬∑C(n,k) for k in 0..n) = n¬≤(n+3)¬∑2‚Åø; the third binomial moment ‚àëk¬≥C(n,k) = n¬≤(n+3)2^(n‚àí3). | proved | 3 | ‚Äî | classic identities (binomial-moment tower ‚Äî compounds on the proved `sum-range-sq-mul-choose`) | The third moment of the binomial distribution scaled by 2‚Åø; Graham, Knuth & Patashnik, Concrete Mathematics, Ch. 5 (binomial coefficients / generating-function moments); Riordan, Combinatorial Identities. |
| `sum-range-cube-odd` ‚Äî For every natural n, the sum of the cubes of the first n odd numbers equals n^2 * (2n^2 - 1); i.e. 1^3 + 3^3 + ... + (2n-1)^3 = n^2(2n^2 - 1). | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | classic identities | Standard finite-sum identity ‚àë(2k-1)^3 = n^2(2n^2-1); CRC Standard Mathematical Tables (sums of powers of odd integers); Gradshteyn & Ryzhik, Table of Integrals, Series, and Products (sums section). |
| `sum-range-fall-mul-choose` ‚Äî For every natural n, 4¬∑(sum of k(k‚àí1)¬∑C(n,k) for k in 0..n) = n(n‚àí1)¬∑2‚Åø; the second falling-factorial moment ‚àëk(k‚àí1)C(n,k) = n(n‚àí1)2^(n‚àí2). | proved | 3 | ‚Äî | classic identities (binomial-moment tower) | Second factorial moment of the binomial distribution; from double absorption k(k‚àí1)C(n,k) = n(n‚àí1)C(n‚àí2,k‚àí2). Graham, Knuth & Patashnik, Concrete Mathematics, Ch. 5. |
| `sum-range-fib-even-index` ‚Äî For every natural n, ‚àë_{i<n} F(2(i+1)) = F(2n+1) ‚àí 1: the sum of the first n even-indexed Fibonacci numbers F‚ÇÇ + F‚ÇÑ + ‚ãØ + F_{2n} equals F(2n+1) ‚àí 1. | proved | 3 | ‚Äî | Fibonacci identities | Vajda (1989), identity (6); Koshy, Thm 5.2. Companion to the odd-index sum `sum-range-fib-odd-index`, whose result it can reuse. |
| `sum-range-fib-odd-index` ‚Äî For every natural n, ‚àë_{i<n} F(2i+1) = F(2n): the sum of the first n odd-indexed Fibonacci numbers F‚ÇÅ + F‚ÇÉ + ‚ãØ + F_{2n‚àí1} equals F(2n). | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | Fibonacci identities | Vajda, Fibonacci & Lucas Numbers and the Golden Section (1989), identity (5); Koshy, Fibonacci and Lucas Numbers with Applications, Thm 5.1. mathlib has single-term `Nat.fib_two_mul` and the all-index `Nat.fib_succ_eq_succ_sum`, but no odd-indexed Fibonacci sum. |
| `sum-range-fib-sq` ‚Äî For every natural n, the sum over i in 0..n of (fib i)^2 equals fib n * fib (n+1) (the telescoping identity F_0^2+...+F_n^2 = F_n F_{n+1}). | proved | 2 | [packet-ready](upstream/sum-range-fib-sq.md) | classic identities | Standard Fibonacci telescoping identity; Koshy, Fibonacci and Lucas Numbers with Applications, ¬ß5; Vajda, Fibonacci & Lucas Numbers, and the Golden Section. |
| `sum-range-id-eq-choose-two` ‚Äî For every natural n, ‚àë_{i<n} i = C(n, 2): the Gauss sum 0 + 1 + ‚ãØ + (n‚àí1) equals the binomial coefficient 'n choose 2'. | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | Gauss summation as a binomial coefficient | The handshake identity ‚àëk = C(n,2); Graham, Knuth & Patashnik, Concrete Mathematics, ¬ß1. mathlib has `Finset.sum_range_id` and `Nat.choose_two_right` as separate closed forms (both n(n-1)/2) but not the theorem equating them. |
| `sum-range-mul-two-pow` ‚Äî For every natural n, the sum over i in 0..n-1 of i * 2^i equals (n - 2) * 2^n + 2 (stated over ‚Ñ§, where the right side is negative-free for all n). | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | classic identities | Arithmetico-geometric sum ‚àëk¬∑2^k = (n-2)¬∑2^n + 2; Graham, Knuth & Patashnik, Concrete Mathematics, ¬ß2.5 (the perturbation-method worked example ‚àëk¬∑2^k). |
| `sum-range-odd-eq-sq` ‚Äî For every natural n, the sum over i in 0..n-1 of (2i+1) equals n^2 (i.e. 1+3+5+...+(2n-1) = n^2). | proved | 1 | [packet-ready](upstream/sum-range-odd-eq-sq.md) | classic identities | Classical 'gnomon' identity (sum of consecutive odd numbers is a perfect square). Graham, Knuth & Patashnik, Concrete Mathematics, 2nd ed., ¬ß2.5; Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, 7th‚Ä¶ |
| `sum-range-pentagonal-closed-form` ‚Äî Closed form for the sum of the first n pentagonal numbers: $2 \sum_{k=0}^n \frac{3k^2-k}{2} = n^2(n+1)$. | proved | 3 | ‚Äî | classic figural number identities | Pentagonal number theorem for partial sums of pentagonal numbers. |
| `sum-range-pow-five-add-pow-seven` ‚Äî For every natural n, (sum of i‚Åµ for i in 0..n) + (sum of i‚Å∑ for i in 0..n) = 2¬∑(sum of i for i in 0..n)‚Å¥; i.e. ‚àëk‚Åµ + ‚àëk‚Å∑ = 2(‚àëk)‚Å¥ = 2T‚Å¥ where T = n(n+1)/2 is the n-th triangular number. | proved | 4 | ‚Äî | classic identities (power-sum tower ‚Äî the **crown**: compounds on `sum-range-pow-five-closed-form` + `sum-range-pow-seven-closed-form`) | A classic Faulhaber curiosity: the sum of the fifth- and seventh-power sums is exactly twice the fourth power of the triangular number, generalising Nicomachus's ‚àëk¬≥ = (‚àëk)¬≤ = T¬≤ one octave up. Verified ‚àÄ n; see Knuth, "Johann Faulhaber and sums of powers", Math. Comp. 61 (1993) for the triangular-number structure of odd-power sums. |
| `sum-range-pow-five-closed-form` ‚Äî For every natural n, 12 * (sum of i^5 for i in 0..n) = n^2 (n+1)^2 (2n^2 + 2n - 1); Faulhaber's closed form for fifth powers, ‚àëk^5 = (2n^6 + 6n^5 + 5n^4 - n^2)/12. | proved | 3 | ‚Äî | classic identities | Faulhaber's formula, p = 5 case; Conway & Guy, The Book of Numbers (sums of powers); D. E. Knuth, "Johann Faulhaber and sums of powers", Math. Comp. 61 (1993); CRC Standard Mathematical Tables. |
| `sum-range-pow-five-faulhaber-triangular` ‚Äî For every natural n, 3¬∑(sum of i‚Åµ for i in 0..n) = (sum of i for i in 0..n)¬≤¬∑(4¬∑(sum of i for i in 0..n)‚àí1); i.e. ‚àëk‚Åµ = T¬≤(4T‚àí1)/3 where T = ‚àëk. Faulhaber's theorem made concrete: the fifth-power sum is a pure polynomial in the triangular number T. | proved | 3 | ‚Äî | classic identities (Faulhaber-in-T tower ‚Äî odd-power rung; compounds on `sum-range-pow-five-closed-form`) | Faulhaber's 1631 result that odd-power sums are polynomials in T = n(n+1)/2: ‚àëk‚Åµ = (4T¬≥‚àíT¬≤)/3 = T¬≤(4T‚àí1)/3. Knuth, "Johann Faulhaber and sums of powers", Math. Comp. 61 (1993). |
| `sum-range-pow-four-closed-form` ‚Äî For every natural n, 30 * (sum of k^4 for k in 0..n) = n(n+1)(2n+1)(3n^2+3n-1), the integer (‚Ñ§) form of the Faulhaber p=4 identity. | proved | 2 | [packet-ready](upstream/sum-range-pow-four-closed-form.md) | classic identities | Faulhaber's formula, case p=4. Conway & Guy, The Book of Numbers, Ch. 2; Knuth, 'Johann Faulhaber and sums of powers', Math. Comp. 61 (1993). |
| `sum-range-pow-four-triangular-form` ‚Äî For every natural n, 15¬∑(sum of i‚Å¥ for i in 0..n) = (sum of i for i in 0..n)¬∑(2n+1)¬∑(3n¬≤+3n‚àí1); i.e. ‚àëk‚Å¥ = T(2n+1)(3n¬≤+3n‚àí1)/15 where T = ‚àëk. | proved | 3 | ‚Äî | classic identities (Faulhaber-in-T tower ‚Äî even-power rung) | Faulhaber's theorem, even-power case: ‚àëk‚Å¥ = n(n+1)(2n+1)(3n¬≤+3n‚àí1)/30 = T(2n+1)(3n¬≤+3n‚àí1)/15. Knuth, "Johann Faulhaber and sums of powers", Math. Comp. 61 (1993); CRC Standard Mathematical Tables. |
| `sum-range-pow-seven-closed-form` ‚Äî For every natural n, 24¬∑(sum of i‚Å∑ for i in 0..n) = n¬≤(n+1)¬≤(3n‚Å¥+6n¬≥‚àín¬≤‚àí4n+2); Faulhaber's closed form for seventh powers, ‚àëk‚Å∑ = (3n‚Å∏+12n‚Å∑+14n‚Å∂‚àí7n‚Å¥+2n¬≤)/24. | proved | 4 | ‚Äî | classic identities (power-sum tower ‚Äî the harder odd-power rung) | Faulhaber's formula, p = 7; Conway & Guy, The Book of Numbers; D. E. Knuth, "Johann Faulhaber and sums of powers", Math. Comp. 61 (1993). Faulhaber's own result: odd-power sums are polynomials in the triangular number T = n(n+1)/2. |
| `sum-range-pow-seven-faulhaber-triangular` ‚Äî For every natural n, 3¬∑(sum of i‚Å∑ for i in 0..n) = (sum of i for i in 0..n)¬≤¬∑(6¬∑(sum of i for i in 0..n)¬≤‚àí4¬∑(sum of i for i in 0..n)+1); i.e. ‚àëk‚Å∑ = T¬≤(6T¬≤‚àí4T+1)/3 where T = ‚àëk. The seventh-power sum as a pure polynomial in the triangular number. | proved | 4 | ‚Äî | classic identities (Faulhaber-in-T tower ‚Äî the **capstone** odd-power rung; compounds on `sum-range-pow-seven-closed-form`) | Faulhaber's theorem for p=7: ‚àëk‚Å∑ = (6T‚Å¥‚àí4T¬≥+T¬≤)/3 = T¬≤(6T¬≤‚àí4T+1)/3. Knuth, "Johann Faulhaber and sums of powers", Math. Comp. 61 (1993). |
| `sum-range-pow-six-closed-form` ‚Äî For every natural n, 42¬∑(sum of i‚Å∂ for i in 0..n) = n(n+1)(2n+1)(3n‚Å¥+6n¬≥‚àí3n+1); Faulhaber's closed form for sixth powers, ‚àëk‚Å∂ = (6n‚Å∑+21n‚Å∂+21n‚Åµ‚àí7n¬≥+n)/42. | proved | 3 | ‚Äî | classic identities (power-sum tower ‚Äî the next rung above proved p=2..p=5) | Faulhaber's formula, p = 6; Conway & Guy, The Book of Numbers (sums of powers); D. E. Knuth, "Johann Faulhaber and sums of powers", Math. Comp. 61 (1993); CRC Standard Mathematical Tables. |
| `sum-range-pronic` ‚Äî For every natural n, 3 * (sum of i(i+1) for i in 0..n) = n(n+1)(n+2); the sum of the first pronic (oblong) numbers, equivalently 2 * C(n+2, 3). | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | classic identities | Pronic/oblong number sums ‚àëk(k+1) = n(n+1)(n+2)/3; Graham, Knuth & Patashnik, Concrete Mathematics, Ch. 2; CRC Standard Mathematical Tables (figurate numbers). |
| `sum-range-recip-pronic` ‚Äî For every natural n, the sum over i in 0..n-1 of 1/((i+1)(i+2)) equals n/(n+1) (over ‚Ñö); the classic telescoping sum 1/(1¬∑2) + 1/(2¬∑3) + ... + 1/(n(n+1)) = n/(n+1). | proved | 3 | ‚Äî | classic identities | Classic telescoping/partial-fractions identity ‚àë 1/(k(k+1)) = n/(n+1); Graham, Knuth & Patashnik, Concrete Mathematics, ¬ß2.5 (partial fractions); standard first-year analysis exercise. |
| `sum-range-recip-triangular` ‚Äî For every natural n, ‚àë_{k<n} 2/((k+1)(k+2)) = 2n/(n+1) over ‚Ñö: the reciprocals of the triangular numbers telescope (‚àë 1/T_k ‚Üí 2). | proved | 3 | ‚Äî | telescoping series (reciprocals of triangular numbers) | Standard telescoping series; the sum of reciprocals of all triangular numbers is 2. Companion to `sum-range-recip-pronic` (1/(k(k+1))) already in the library, sharing the partial-fraction machinery over ‚Ñö. |
| `sum-range-sq-closed-form` ‚Äî For every natural n, 6 * (sum of i^2 for i in 0..n) = n(n+1)(2n+1), the integer form of 1^2+...+n^2 = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6. | proved | 2 | [packet-ready](upstream/sum-range-sq-closed-form.md) | classic identities | Faulhaber's formula, case p=2 (square pyramidal number). Apostol, Calculus Vol. 1, 2nd ed., ¬ßI.4.2; Graham, Knuth & Patashnik, Concrete Mathematics, ¬ß2.5; Avigad/Massot, Mathematics in Lean, ¬ß5‚Ä¶ |
| `sum-range-sq-even` ‚Äî For every natural n, 3 * (sum of (2i)^2 for i in 0..n-1) = 2n(n-1)(2n-1); the sum of the squares of the first n even numbers 0^2 + 2^2 + ... + (2n-2)^2 in closed form. | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | classic identities | Even-square sums ‚àë(2k)^2 = 2m(m+1)(2m+1)/3 (here reindexed from 0); CRC Standard Mathematical Tables (sums of powers); companion of the proved sum-range-sq-odd-closed-form. |
| `sum-range-sq-mul-choose` ‚Äî Weighted sum of squares of binomial coefficients: $4 \sum_{k=0}^n k^2 \binom{n}{k} = n(n+1)2^n$. | proved | 3 | ‚Äî | classic combinatorial identities | Standard combinatorial identity for the second moment of binomial coefficients, derived from differentiating the binomial theorem twice. |
| `sum-range-sq-odd-closed-form` ‚Äî For every natural n, 3 * (sum of (2i+1)^2 for i in 0..n-1) = n(2n-1)(2n+1); i.e. 1^2+3^2+...+(2n-1)^2 = n(2n-1)(2n+1)/3. | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | classic identities | Standard finite-sum identity ‚àë(2k-1)^2 = n(2n-1)(2n+1)/3; Concrete Mathematics ¬ß2.5 exercises; Gradshteyn & Ryzhik, Table of Integrals, Series, and Products (sums section). |
| `sum-range-sq-triangular-form` ‚Äî For every natural n, 3¬∑(sum of i¬≤ for i in 0..n) = (sum of i for i in 0..n)¬∑(2n+1); i.e. ‚àëk¬≤ = T¬∑(2n+1)/3 where T = ‚àëk = n(n+1)/2 is the n-th triangular number. | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | classic identities (Faulhaber-in-T tower ‚Äî power sums as polynomials in the triangular number) | Faulhaber's theorem: ‚àëk·µñ is a polynomial in T = n(n+1)/2; the even-power cases carry a factor (2n+1). ‚àëk¬≤ = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6 = T(2n+1)/3. Knuth, "Johann Faulhaber and sums of powers", Math. Comp. 61 (1993). |
| `sum-range-three-consecutive-product` ‚Äî For every natural n, 4¬∑‚àë_{i<n} i(i+1)(i+2) = (n‚àí1)¬∑n¬∑(n+1)¬∑(n+2): the telescoping sum of products of three consecutive integers (the tetrahedral-by-4 closed form). | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | falling-factorial telescoping | Graham, Knuth & Patashnik, Concrete Mathematics, ¬ß2.6 (summation by parts on falling factorials); ‚àë_{i=1}^{m} i(i+1)(i+2) = m(m+1)(m+2)(m+3)/4. |
| `three-cubes-div-nine` ‚Äî For every natural n, 9 divides n^3 + (n+1)^3 + (n+2)^3; the sum of any three consecutive cubes is divisible by 9. | proved | 2 | ‚Äî | classic identities | Classic introductory number-theory / olympiad exercise; Engel, Problem-Solving Strategies (divisibility chapter); Sierpi≈Ñski, Elementary Theory of Numbers (PWN/North-Holland, 1988). |
